story_clusters.headline: Vijay convenes crucial meet of TVK MLAs-designate
story_clusters.summary: After the Tamil Nadu Assembly Election Results, TVK founder Vijay convened a crucial meeting with his party’s MLAs-designate, while DMK leader MK Stalin declared his party’s "trust undefeated" and vowed to win again. Additionally, TVK secured a historic victory, winning over 100 seats in the Tamil Nadu Assembly Election Results 2026. Separately, sources reported on the Kerala Assembly Election Results 2026, including the date, time, and where to check the results.

story_clusters.headline: Assembly elections: Kerala Congress desires at least two ministerial berths in UDF govt, says P.J. Joseph
story_clusters.summary: Kerala Congress has requested at least two ministerial berths in the United Democratic Front (UDF) government following the assembly elections, with party leader P.J. Joseph stating the issue will be resolved amicably. Meanwhile, the BJP-led NDA secured a majority in Assam, winning 102 seats in the 126-member Assembly. Additionally, the BJP's victory in West Bengal is expected to lead to a renewed focus on central schemes in the state.

story_clusters.summary: Multiple sources report that tensions involving Iran, including potential conflict in the Strait of Hormuz, are raising concerns about economic and geopolitical risks. Analysts warn that an Iran-related conflict could lead to prolonged inflation shocks, while oil prices have fluctuated amid diplomatic efforts to ease tensions. Investors are closely monitoring developments, with precious metals like silver and gold seeing price movements tied to the situation.

Reports note that the armed forces have fast-tracked priorities, including underground infrastructure and air defense, in the year since the operation began.

Some sources indicate a potential weakening trend, with projections suggesting the rupee could trade around ₹95 per US dollar by 2026-end amid ongoing Iran-related risks. However, one report notes a brief rise of 23 paise to 94.95 in early trade.

story_clusters.summary: The Indian government has issued updated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) to streamline Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) approvals, introducing a paperless process and a 12-week timeline for general cases. For China-linked sectors, a 60-day clearance period has been set across 40 sub-sectors. The changes, issued by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), aim to make FDI approvals more efficient.

story_clusters.summary: Crude oil prices remained steady around $102 per barrel as investors assessed the potential for a US-Iran peace deal, with Brent crude slumping on hopes of reduced tensions. Meanwhile, Indian stock markets surged, with the Sensex and Nifty rising, and the Nikkei breaching the 62,000 mark.

articles.summary: Petitioners told the Supreme Court that the Election Commissioners Act, 2023, ensures the Prime Minister's suggested candidates are appointed as Election Commissioners, allegedly compromising the Election Commission's independence. The petitioners argue the Act makes it impossible to select alternative candidates, raising concerns about the body's autonomy.

articles.title: Gujarat HC Upholds ITC Denial on Supplier Default
articles.summary: The Gujarat High Court upheld Section 16(2)(c) of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act, allowing the denial of Input Tax Credit (ITC) to businesses if their suppliers fail to deposit GST with the government. This ruling impacts tax compliance and the availability of ITC for taxpayers. The decision reinforces the government's stance on preventing tax evasion through supplier defaults.

articles.title: Political Donation Reassessment Notice Quashed as Issued by Wrong Authority
articles.summary: A tribunal invalidated a tax reassessment notice under Section 148 because it was issued by an officer lacking jurisdiction. The ruling relied on CBDT instructions that set monetary limits for such notices. This highlights the strict adherence required to jurisdictional norms in tax administration. The decision underscores the importance of proper authority in issuing tax notices.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that notice under Section 148 was invalid as it was issued by an officer lacking jurisdiction. It relied on CBDT Instruction prescribing monetary limits. The ruling highlights strict adherence to jurisdictional norms. The post Political Donation Reassessment Notice Quashed as Issued by Wrong Authority appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Constitutional Infirmity of Retrospective Section 147A of Income Tax Act, 1961
articles.summary: The article examines whether the retrospective application of Section 147A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, is constitutionally valid. It argues that legislatures cannot retroactively alter past legal interpretations settled by courts under the guise of clarification. The analysis focuses on constitutional limits to retrospective tax laws and their impact on judicial precedents.
articles.raw_summary: Examines whether retrospective tax laws can validly alter past legal interpretations. The case highlights how constitutional limits prevent legislatures from rewriting judicially settled meanings under the guise of clarification. The post Constitutional Infirmity of Retrospective Section 147A of Income Tax Act, 1961 appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Bombay HC Orders ITC Unblocking as Rule 86A Restriction Expired After One Year
articles.summary: The Bombay High Court ruled that the blocking of electronic credit ledgers under Rule 86A of India's tax laws cannot continue beyond the statutory one-year period. The restriction automatically lapses by law, even if departmental proceedings are pending. This decision impacts businesses and tax administration, clarifying that such blocks must be lifted after one year. The court emphasized that the restriction is time-bound and not indefinite.
articles.raw_summary: The Court held that blocking of the electronic credit ledger under Rule 86A cannot continue beyond the statutory one-year period. It clarified that such restriction automatically lapses by operation of law regardless of pending departmental proceedings. The post Bombay HC Orders ITC Unblocking as Rule 86A Restriction Expired After One Year appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Legal Analysis of Applicability of GST on Bar Associations
articles.summary: A legal analysis explains how India's GST law treats bar associations and their members as separate taxable entities, making internal transactions such as membership fees subject to GST. This ruling overrides the principle of mutuality, where associations and members were previously considered one unit for tax purposes. The decision has significant implications for how professional associations manage their tax compliance and financial operations under India's indirect tax regime.
articles.raw_summary: This case explains how GST law overrides mutuality by treating associations and members as distinct taxable persons. It clarifies that internal transactions like membership fees now qualify as taxable supply. The post Legal Analysis of Applicability of GST on Bar Associations appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Summary Judgment in Commercial Suits: Supreme Court Clarifies Scope under Order XIII-A CPC
articles.summary: The Supreme Court of India has issued clear guidelines for summary judgment under Order XIII-A of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) in commercial disputes. The ruling limits the scope of trials to cases where no genuine defense exists, aiming to expedite commercial litigation. This decision impacts how commercial suits are handled in Indian courts, ensuring faster resolution where defenses lack merit.

articles.title: Miscellaneous Application Rejected as Tribunal Lacks Power to Review Its Own Order
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) dismissed a revenue application, ruling it lacked the power to review its own order. The tribunal clarified that challenges to evidence appreciation do not qualify as rectification under Section 254(2), which only allows correction of apparent errors. This decision impacts how tax disputes are handled and sets a precedent for tribunal jurisdiction.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that challenges to appreciation of evidence amount to review, not rectification. It ruled that Section 254(2) permits only correction of apparent errors, leading to dismissal of the Revenue’s application. The post Miscellaneous Application Rejected as Tribunal Lacks Power to Review Its Own Order appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: No Section 68 Addition Where Loan Repaid Through Banking Channels & Supported by Evidence
articles.summary: A tribunal ruled that no tax addition under Section 68 can be made if loans to related parties are repaid through banking channels and supported by evidence. The decision emphasized that interest cannot be disallowed when advances are made for business purposes using own funds, upholding the principle of commercial expediency. The ruling impacts how businesses document inter-corporate loans for tax compliance.
articles.raw_summary: The tribunal held that interest cannot be disallowed where advances to related parties are made for business purposes and out of own funds, emphasizing the principle of commercial expediency. The move is expected to improve efficiency and accessibility in judicial processes. The CJI highlighted this as a pioneering effort for other states to emulate.

articles.title: Section 263 Invalid as AO Conducted Proper Inquiry & Took Plausible View: ITAT Surat
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Surat ruled that Section 263 of the Income Tax Act cannot be used to override the assessment officer's (AO) independent decision if it was based on a proper inquiry and a plausible view. The tribunal emphasized that revisionary powers under Section 263 do not allow the Principal Commissioner of Income Tax (Pr. CIT) to substitute their judgment for that of the AO. This decision reinforces the autonomy of tax assessment processes.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that revisionary powers cannot be used to substitute the AO’s view with that of the Pr. CIT. It emphasized that such substitution is beyond Section 263. The decision protects independent assessment decisions. The post Section 263 Invalid as AO Conducted Proper Inquiry   Took Plausible View: ITAT Surat appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: From 33 to 38 judges—Union Cabinet approves plan to increase seats on Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Union Cabinet has approved a plan to increase the number of judges in the Supreme Court from 33 to 38. The central government will introduce The Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026 in Parliament. The last increase in the court's strength was from 30 to 33 in 2019.
articles.raw_summary: Central government will now introduce The Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026 in Parliament. Top court’s strength was last increased from 30 to 33 in 2019.

articles.title: Allahabad HC Quashed GST Penalty as Wrong Provision Invoked for Excess Stock During Survey
articles.summary: The Allahabad High Court set aside a GST penalty imposed on a taxpayer for excess stock found during a survey. The court ruled that such cases should be handled under Sections 73/74 of the GST law, not Section 130, making the penalty order unsustainable. This judgment clarifies the correct legal framework for addressing excess stock issues during surveys, impacting future tax enforcement actions.
articles.raw_summary: The Court examined whether Section 130 proceedings were valid when excess stock was found during a survey. It held that such cases must be dealt with under Sections 73/74, rendering the penalty order unsustainable. The post Allahabad HC Quashed GST Penalty as Wrong Provision Invoked for Excess Stock During Survey appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Why Madras HC ordered granting full maternity leave to judicial employee for third pregnancy
articles.summary: The Madras High Court ruled that a judicial employee should receive full maternity leave for her third pregnancy, criticizing a 12-week limit as discriminatory. The court stated that suffering during pregnancy is the same regardless of the number of pregnancies and that a welfare state cannot deny equal benefits. The ruling highlights the need for equitable maternity leave policies in government employment.
articles.raw_summary: The court criticised a 12-week maternity leave limit on third pregnancies, saying 'suffering would be the same' and a 'welfare state' cannot discriminate in approving maternity benefits.

articles.summary: The Bombay High Court ruled that tax reassessment based on a change of opinion using existing records is invalid after four years. The court quashed such reassessment, reinforcing limits on the government's power to reopen cases. This decision impacts tax administration and reassessment procedures in India. The ruling was made in response to a legal challenge against reassessment proceedings.

articles.summary: The Supreme Court dismissed a penalty case involving a dispute over whether a subsidy should be classified as a capital or revenue receipt. The Court ruled that such classification issues are debatable and do not amount to furnishing inaccurate particulars, reinforcing limits on penalties under tax law. This decision impacts how tax authorities can impose penalties in similar cases. The ruling was made in response to a tax dispute where the classification of subsidy receipts was contested.

articles.title: RSP-based Valuation under GST: Constitutional Limits & Structural Incompatibility
articles.summary: The article examines the constitutional validity of taxing goods based on their retail sale price (RSP) under the Goods and Services Tax (GST) instead of actual transaction values. It argues that such valuation lacks a direct connection to the supply of goods and may violate constitutional principles governing tax measures. The analysis highlights structural incompatibilities in the proposed approach, raising concerns about its legal and policy implications for India's tax system.
articles.raw_summary: The issue concerns taxing goods based on retail price instead of actual transaction value. It highlights that such valuation lacks nexus with supply and may violate constitutional principles governing tax measures. The post RSP-based Valuation under GST: Constitutional Limits   Structural Incompatibility appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.summary: The Andhra Pradesh High Court ruled that GST on solar power projects cannot be taxed at a flat 18% due to the 70:30 valuation mechanism. The court held that separate invoicing does not disqualify solar supplies from concessional tax treatment. This decision impacts tax compliance and incentives for India's solar energy sector.

articles.title: No Evidence of Rowdyism: Karnataka HC Strikes Down Police Register Entry
articles.summary: The Karnataka High Court ruled that including a person in a 'rowdy register' without evidence of criminal activity or supporting material is unlawful. The court ordered the deletion of such entries, emphasizing that police must have tangible proof before listing individuals. The ruling impacts police procedures and individual rights against arbitrary listings.
articles.raw_summary: The Court examined inclusion in a rowdy register without supporting material and found no criminal cases or evidence. It held that such listing is unlawful without tangible proof and ordered deletion of the entry. articles.title: No Substantial Question of Law Where AO Accepts Claims in Remand: Karnataka HC
articles.summary: The Karnataka High Court ruled that no substantial question of law remains when the Assessing Officer (AO) accepts claims during a remand process in a tax case. The revenue had challenged multiple additions granted to the assessee, but the Court held that the AO's acceptance of claims in remand negates the need for further legal scrutiny. This decision impacts how tax disputes are handled in remand proceedings.
articles.raw_summary: The case involved multiple additions challenged by the revenue after relief was granted to the assessee. The Court held that once the Assessing Officer accepted the claims in remand, no substantial question of law survives. The post No Substantial Question of Law Where AO Accepts Claims in Remand: Karnataka HC appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: When Myanmar Exported Federalism
articles.summary: After World War II, Myanmar (then Burma) helped draft a federal constitution for Ethiopia and Eritrea while simultaneously fighting a civil war with ethnic minorities demanding federalism at home. This historical case highlights the complexities of federal governance and ethnic minority rights, which remain relevant to discussions on federalism and governance in India and globally.

Currently, insults to these symbols can lead to imprisonment of up to three years or a fine. The move aims to strengthen respect for national symbols. The proposal is part of a broader legal framework addressing disrespect to national icons.

articles.title: Section 271(1)(c) Penalty Cannot Be Imposed for Valuation Differences Without Proof of Inaccuracy: SC
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that penalties under Section 271(1)(c) of the Income Tax Act cannot be imposed solely due to differences in property valuation unless there is proof of deliberate inaccuracy. The court clarified that mere estimation disputes do not justify penalties, emphasizing that proof of intentional misrepresentation is required. This ruling impacts tax administration and enforcement practices in India.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court held that mere differences in property valuation do not amount to furnishing inaccurate particulars. Penalty under Section 271(1)(c) requires proof of deliberate inaccuracy. The ruling clarifies that estimation disputes alone cannot justify penalty. articles.title: What the Strait of Hormuz fuel crisis means for air travel
articles.summary: A fuel crisis in the Strait of Hormuz, a key oil shipping route, threatens to disrupt jet fuel supplies and cause major flight cancellations this summer. articles.title: GST Confiscation Proceedings Quashed as Stock Discrepancy Must Be Examined Under Sections 73/74
articles.summary: The Gujarat High Court invalidated GST confiscation proceedings initiated against a taxpayer after excess stock was allegedly found during a factory survey. The court ruled that the proceedings under Section 130 were wrongly initiated and that stock discrepancies must first be examined under Sections 73 or 74 of the GST Act. This decision impacts how tax authorities handle stock-related disputes and confiscation cases under GST.

articles.title: Bombay HC Set Aside GST Refund Rejection for Not Issuing SCN
articles.summary: The Bombay High Court set aside the rejection of a GST refund because the tax authorities failed to issue a show-cause notice (SCN) before denying the refund, as required under Rule 92(3). The Court ruled that the absence of notice and hearing makes the rejection invalid and ordered the authorities to reconsider the refund application afresh. The case highlights the importance of following due process in tax administration.

articles.title: S.28 Specific Relief Act | Separate Application Not Needed To Rescind Agreement To Sell For Buyer's Default  : Suprme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that no separate application is required to rescind an agreement to sell due to a buyer's default under Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act. The judgment clarifies legal principles governing such rescissions, impacting property transactions and contract enforcement in India. The ruling provides clarity on procedural requirements for buyers and sellers in default scenarios.
articles.raw_summary: The judgment summarised the principles relating to Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act.

articles.title: Seized Data Must Be Shared If Relied Upon, Calcutta HC Clarifies in GST Case
articles.summary: The Calcutta High Court ruled that taxpayers must be given access to and copies of documents relied upon in GST notices before they respond. The decision addresses disputes where authorities deny inspection of seized data used in tax demands. This clarifies procedural fairness in GST enforcement. The ruling impacts how tax authorities handle evidence in notices.
articles.raw_summary: The issue involved denial of access to seized documents relied upon in a GST notice. The Court held that taxpayers must be given inspection and copies of relied documents before responding. articles.summary: The High Court restored a taxpayer's right to file a GST appeal after condoning a delay caused by a medical emergency. The court ruled that writ jurisdiction can override limitation periods when sufficient cause is shown. This decision impacts tax governance and the rights of taxpayers facing registration cancellations. The case highlights the judiciary's role in balancing procedural rules with substantive justice in tax matters.
articles.raw_summary: The case involved cancellation of GST registration and delay in filing appeal due to illness. The Court held that writ jurisdiction can override limitation where sufficient cause is shown and restored the appeal for decision on merits. articles.title: “Not a single whisper of force”: Bombay HC Quashes POCSO Case After Accused Marries Minor Prosecutrix, Now 7 Months Pregnant
articles.summary: The Bombay High Court quashed a POCSO case after the accused married the minor prosecutrix, who is now seven months pregnant. The court held that no force or coercion was involved and that the victim had no issue staying with the accused and his family. The ruling highlights the legal complexities in cases involving minors under the POCSO Act. The decision underscores the need for careful application of child protection laws in such circumstances.
articles.raw_summary: There was not a single whisper that the accused forced the prosecutrix or threatened her or had sexual intercourse on false promise of marriage. Though the victim was minor, she did not have any problem in staying with the accused and his family. Therefore, the Court held that the criminal proceedings would cause injustice. articles.title: Jharkhand HC Dismissed Writ Petition Due to Availability of Alternate GST Appeal Remedy
articles.summary: The Jharkhand High Court dismissed a writ petition filed by local self-government bodies seeking GST exemptions, stating that such claims must go through the statutory appellate mechanism rather than bypassing it. The court clarified that not all activities by these bodies are automatically exempt from GST and require factual scrutiny. The ruling underscores the need to follow prescribed legal remedies before approaching courts for tax-related disputes.
articles.raw_summary: The Court clarified that local self-government bodies are not automatically exempt from GST on all activities. It ruled that such claims require factual scrutiny and cannot bypass the statutory appellate mechanism. The post Jharkhand HC Dismissed Writ Petition Due to Availability of Alternate GST Appeal Remedy appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: TDS Never Sleeps: How Form 26AS Triggers Tax Scrutiny
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal ruled that TDS entries in Form 26AS are sufficient grounds for reopening tax assessments if no return is filed. Taxpayers must reconcile these entries with their records to avoid reassessment. The ruling underscores the importance of accurate tax compliance and record-keeping.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that TDS entries appearing in Form 26AS are sufficient material for reopening assessments when no return is filed. The ruling emphasizes that taxpayers must reconcile Form 26AS with books and returns to avoid reassessment proceedings. articles.title: CCPA Penalises Seller for Selling Non-BIS Toys Through Snapdeal
articles.summary: The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) penalized a seller for selling toys without mandatory Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) certification through Snapdeal. The CCPA ruled that this violated the Consumer Protection Act and the Toys Quality Control Order, imposing a penalty and directing future compliance with mandatory standards. The decision underscores the enforcement of quality control norms for toys in India.

articles.title: India’s state election results are out. Is cash inflow next? Here’s a warning

articles.title: Karnataka HC Protects Genuine Buyers from ITC Denial Due to Supplier Default
articles.summary: The Karnataka High Court ruled that authorities cannot deny Input Tax Credit (ITC) to genuine buyers solely because their suppliers are tagged as non-genuine taxpayers. The Court emphasized that an 'NGTP' tag is only a risk indicator and not conclusive proof, requiring authorities to conduct thorough investigations and provide transaction-specific findings before rejecting ITC claims. This decision protects buyers from penalties due to supplier defaults and clarifies procedural fairness in tax a
articles.raw_summary: The Court clarified that an NGTP or non-genuine taxpayer tag is only a risk indicator and not conclusive proof against buyers. Authorities must conduct proper enquiry, examine records, and provide transaction-wise findings before rejecting ITC claims. The post Karnataka HC Protects Genuine Buyers from ITC Denial Due to Supplier Default appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Gold rate up amid focus on potential US-Iran peace deal; dimming US Fed rate cut hopes cap gains
articles.summary: Gold prices on the Multi Commodity Exchange (MCX) rose on Thursday morning. The increase is linked to hopes of a US-Iran peace deal, which could reopen the Strait of Hormuz—a critical route for global oil shipments. However, gains were capped by reduced expectations of US Federal Reserve interest rate cuts. The developments may impact global oil supply and prices, indirectly affecting India's energy and trade policies.
articles.raw_summary: Gold rate rose on MCX on Thursday morning. The big relief for gold traders is the prospects of a peace deal between the US and Iran, which can lead to the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil trade.

articles.title: MPs, ex Union ministers must be addressed with ‘Honourable’ prefix, even in FIR. Allahabad HC clarifies
articles.summary: The Allahabad High Court ruled that MPs and former Union ministers must be addressed with the 'Honourable' prefix in all official documents, including First Information Reports (FIRs). The two-judge bench clarified that this requirement does not apply to civil servants, regardless of their seniority. The ruling sets a legal precedent for how elected officials are referred to in formal proceedings.
articles.raw_summary: Two-judge bench however said that civil servants do not have to be addressed in the similar fashion, irrespective of their seniority.

articles.title: Unlisted Bonds Lose LTCG Benefit; No Capital Gains Exemption for Depreciable Assets under Income Tax Act 2025
articles.summary: The Income Tax Act 2025 removes long-term capital gains (LTCG) benefits for unlisted bonds and eliminates exemptions for gains from depreciable assets. Previously, judicial interpretations allowed some exemptions, but the new law treats all such gains as short-term capital gains. This change affects taxpayers holding depreciable assets or unlisted bonds, removing prior tax benefits.
articles.raw_summary: The new law treats gains from depreciable assets as short-term capital gains for all purposes, not merely for computation. This effectively removes exemption benefits previously allowed through judicial interpretation under the old regime. The post Unlisted Bonds Lose LTCG Benefit; No Capital Gains Exemption for Depreciable Assets under Income Tax Act 2025 appeared first on TaxGuru .

This change addresses concerns raised by the Tiger Global ruling, which had increased scrutiny of legacy investments. The amendment provides clearer safeguards for pre-existing investments under GAAR.

articles.title: Ethanol Uses 3–5 Litres of Water, not 10,000; Misinterpreted Data Clouds Energy Security Debate: Grain Ethanol Industry - GEMA
articles.summary: GEMA, representing the grain ethanol industry, has pointed out factual inaccuracies in claims that ethanol production uses 10,000 litres of water per litre. The industry argues that ethanol actually uses only 3–5 litres of water, emphasizing its efficiency, sustainability, and benefits for India's energy security. The article underscores the need for accurate data in the ongoing debate on ethanol blending policies.

This shift is reshaping telecom pricing strategies and subscriber behavior. The article highlights how market dynamics are evolving, which could prompt further regulatory or policy discussions by authorities like TRAI.

articles.title: ADB warns Asian economies against broad fuel subsidies, tax cuts amid oil price risks
articles.summary: The Asian Development Bank (ADB) advised Asian economies, including India, to avoid broad fuel subsidies and tax cuts due to rising crude oil prices, warning they could strain public finances. Instead, it recommended targeted cash transfers for vulnerable groups and a shift toward green energy. The lender also urged central banks to monitor inflation before tightening monetary policy.
articles.raw_summary: The Asian Development Bank advised Asian economies to avoid broad fuel subsidies and excise duty cuts, warning they could strain public finances amid elevated crude oil prices. The lender suggested targeted cash transfers for vulnerable populations and a shift to green energy, while urging central banks to monitor inflation expectations before tightening monetary policy.

articles.title: Teen rape survivor’s choice vs unborn baby’s right to life—how SC’s new order deviates from precedent
articles.summary: The Supreme Court, led by Chief Justice of India Surya Kant, rejected AIIMS' plea to deny a 15-year-old rape survivor the right to terminate her 27-week pregnancy. Earlier court rulings often blocked terminations if the fetus was healthy, but this order deviates from precedent. The decision highlights the conflict between a survivor's choice and the unborn baby's right to life. The case underscores the need for clearer legal frameworks on abortion rights in India.

articles.title: Section 130 Cannot Be Used for Excess Stock Detected in GST Survey: Allahabad HC
articles.summary: The Allahabad High Court ruled that Section 130 of the GST law cannot be used to address excess stock detected during a GST survey. The court clarified that the correct legal route for such discrepancies is through Sections 73 or 74 of the GST Act. This ruling reinforces established legal precedents and has implications for how tax authorities handle stock-related discrepancies in GST surveys.
articles.raw_summary: The Court examined whether Section 130 could be used when discrepancies were found during a GST survey. It held that the correct legal route is Sections 73/74 and reinforced settled precedents. The figures are not directly comparable due to GST rate cuts introduced in September 2025. The revenue performance highlights ongoing fiscal trends under India's Goods and Services Tax regime.
articles.raw_summary: Last April, the Centre and states had collected ₹2.37 trillion in GST revenue before adjusting for refunds, but the figures are not strictly comparable because of the GST cuts in September 2025.

articles.title: Anchor judicial recusal in institutional design
articles.summary: The article argues that judicial recusal—when judges step aside from cases—should not rely solely on individual conscience but be anchored in institutional design. It emphasizes that justice must not only be done but also be seen to be done, highlighting the need for structured guidelines to ensure fairness and transparency in the judiciary. The piece is an opinion piece from HT, focusing on systemic improvements in India's legal framework.

articles.title: Caught Red-Handed Taking Bribe? No Prior Sanction Required Under S. 17A PC Act; Corruption Not an Act of Official Duty: Allahabad HC
articles.summary: The Allahabad High Court ruled that offenses under Section 7 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, and Section 61(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) do not constitute acts in discharge of official duty. Therefore, no prior sanction is required to prosecute individuals caught taking bribes. This decision clarifies the legal framework for anti-corruption prosecutions in India, emphasizing that corruption is not protected as an official act.
articles.raw_summary: The offences under Section 7, Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 and Section 61(2) BNS cannot be said to constitute  acts in discharge of official duty  and, therefore, no sanction is required for those acts. The post Caught Red-Handed Taking Bribe? No Prior Sanction Required Under S. 17A PC Act; Corruption Not an Act of Official Duty: Allahabad HC appeared first on SCC Times .

articles.title: Freebie burden may strain state finances, push borrowing higher: Economists
articles.summary: Economists warn that rising welfare spending by Indian states, including subsidies and freebies, could strain state finances and force cuts to capital expenditure. M R Madhavan, Co-founder of PRS Legislative Research, highlights that a large portion of state revenues is already committed to salaries, pensions, interest payments, and subsidies, leaving little room for discretionary spending. He notes this could ultimately push states to borrow more.
articles.raw_summary: M R Madhavan, Co-founder & Pres, PRS Legislative Research, and who has previously worked as an economist with I-Sec, ICICI and Bank of America, said states may ultimately be forced to squeeze capital expenditure if welfare spending rises while revenues disappoint. He noted that a large portion of state revenues is already committed towards salaries, pensions, interest payments and subsidies, leaving limited room for discretionary spending.

articles.title: Recycled Activewear Reveals the Structural Limits of Fashion’s Sustainability Claims
articles.summary: The article examines the sustainability claims of recycled polyester in activewear, noting that most material still comes from PET drink bottles rather than discarded textiles. This raises concerns about whether fashion brands are truly addressing waste or merely shifting it to other recycling systems. The issue has implications for global textile waste management and could influence future environmental and trade policies in India.
articles.raw_summary: Recycled polyester has become a default sustainability claim across activewear, swimwear and outerwear, but its supply chain is less circular than its labelling suggests. Most of the material still comes from PET drink bottles, not discarded textiles, raising difficult questions about whether fashion is solving waste or diverting it from better-functioning recycling systems globally instead.

The situation highlights risks to global oil supply chains, which could impact India's energy imports.

But so far, very few ships appear to want to run the Iranian gauntlet in the strait. Nick Schifrin reports.

articles.title: Welfare spending surge pushes subsidy outgo to 91% of target
articles.summary: The Indian government's welfare spending has surged, with food subsidies reaching ₹1.92 trillion (85% of the revised target) and urea subsidies exceeding the annual target at ₹1.28 trillion (101% of revised estimates). Urea subsidy outgo is also higher than the previous year's ₹1.16 trillion. This reflects increased expenditure on social welfare programs, particularly food and agricultural subsidies, which may prompt policy reviews or adjustments.
articles.raw_summary: Food subsidy, the largest component, stood at ₹1.92 trillion, or 85% of the revised estimate of ₹2.28 trillion. However, the urea subsidy has already breached its annual target, reaching ₹1.28 trillion, or 101% of the revised estimates, and higher than the ₹1.16 trillion recorded a year ago.

articles.title: TVK short of majority; Vijay’s swearing-in under doubt as Governor seeks numbers
articles.summary: Tamil Nadu Governor Rajendra Vishwanath Arlekar has delayed the swearing-in of TVK chief Vijay as Chief Minister, citing a lack of proof of majority support among MLAs. TVK and its allies currently fall short of the required 118 MLAs, raising uncertainty over the government formation process. The Governor's action highlights a constitutional process to ensure stability before formalizing leadership.

articles.summary: Is AI really going to wipe out jobs? Is India’s EdTech sector broken? And can traditional employment still solve India’s employment crisis?On this episode of Young Turks Reloaded, Ronnie Screwvala—media baron turned education investor—joins Shereen Bhan for a wide-ranging conversation on the future of work, entrepreneurship, AI and India’s skilling challenge. articles.title: State-run energy firms can shift over ₹2 trillion annually to clean energy: Report
articles.summary: In FY25, nine state-run energy companies invested ₹2.6 trillion, with ₹2.3 trillion going to fossil fuels and only ₹0.3 trillion to clean energy. The report suggests these firms could shift over ₹2 trillion annually toward clean energy. The data highlights the current dominance of fossil fuel investments and the potential for policy-driven shifts toward renewable energy sources.
articles.raw_summary: In FY25, the nine PSUs together invested ₹2.6 trillion across fossil fuels and clean energy, of this, ₹2.3 trillion was directed towards fossil fuels, compared to about ₹0.3 trillion in clean energy.

The project aligns with India's broader energy transition and renewable energy expansion efforts.

articles.title: Sugarcane FRP hiked to Rs 365/quintal for 2026-27 season
articles.summary: The Indian government has increased the Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for sugarcane to ₹365 per quintal for the 2026-27 season. Farmers will receive an additional ₹3.56 per quintal for every 0.1% increase in sugar recovery above this level, with reductions for lower recovery. This decision impacts sugarcane farmers and the sugar industry, aligning pricing with productivity incentives.
articles.raw_summary: Farmers will receive a premium of ₹3.56 per quintal for every 0.1% increase in recovery above this level, with a corresponding reduction for lower recovery.

articles.title: Services, retail lending drives banks’ credit growth to nearly 16% in FY26
articles.summary: India's banking sector saw credit growth of nearly 16% in FY26, driven by strong retail and services lending. Personal loans grew 16.2%, supported by vehicle financing and gold-backed loans, while housing credit remained stable. Personal loans accounted for about a third of total credit. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) may monitor these trends for potential policy adjustments.
articles.raw_summary: Retail lending remained strong, with personal loans rising 16.2%, up from 11.7% a year earlier, supported by demand for vehicle financing and gold-backed loans, while housing credit growth remained steady. Personal loans accounted for about a third of total credit.

articles.title: Another day, another pivot as Trump flails in an Iran trap of his own making
articles.summary: Donald Trump proposed a new plan to ease tensions with Iran, framed as a humanitarian gesture to allow trapped ships to leave the Gulf. The proposal was criticized by Tehran as unrealistic and comes amid ongoing conflict with high casualties. The plan also aims to counter Iran's control over the Strait of Hormuz, a critical oil shipping route. The U.S. has previously insisted Iran must face greater consequences for its actions.
articles.raw_summary: Latest proposal, derided by Tehran as a ‘wishlist’, could yet be a way out – but with 5,000 dead, it comes at an awful price Another day, another hairpin turn in the world of Donald Trump’s foreign policy. The weekend was all about war, and Trump insisting Iran had not yet “paid a big enough price”. Tuesday was Project Freedom, styled as a grand “humanitarian gesture” to allow trapped ships and their crews to escape the Gulf, but also aimed at weakening Iran’s chokehold on the strait of Hormuz.

articles.title: Kaziranga hosts a healthy population of fishing cats, finds survey

The findings could inform conservation policies or habitat protection measures for endangered species.

The move reflects broader industry trends toward cloud-based AI solutions rather than on-premise hardware or proprietary models.

This decision highlights the need for policy reforms to address gaps in disability rights protections.

articles.title: Oil slips after Trump says US will help free ships stranded in Strait of Hormuz
articles.summary: Oil prices fell after U.S. President Trump announced efforts to free ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz amid ongoing U.S.-Iran tensions. Brent crude dropped to $107.53 and U.S. West Texas Intermediate fell to $101.10, though prices remained above $100 due to stalled peace negotiations. The situation highlights potential risks to global oil supply chains, which could impact India's energy imports and economic stability.
articles.raw_summary: Oil prices fell on Monday after President Trump announced efforts to free stranded ships in the Strait of Hormuz, yet remained above $100 due to ongoing U.S.-Iran tensions. articles.summary: The article examines the decline in Muslim representation in the Trinamool Congress (TMC) party, suggesting that shifts in Muslim voter demographics on the electoral roll may have contributed to the party's poor performance in recent elections. It highlights the role of Muslim voters in electoral outcomes and raises questions about minority participation in political processes. The analysis focuses on demographic changes rather than direct policy actions.
articles.raw_summary: Muslims on the electoral roll, rather than those who were possibly disenfranchised because of SIR, might have a bigger role in the TMC’s poor showing this time

articles.title: US disables Iran oil tanker attempting to break Hormuz blockade
articles.summary: A US warplane launched from the aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln disabled an Iranian oil tanker attempting to break a blockade near the Strait of Hormuz. The incident highlights tensions in the region, which could impact global oil supplies and maritime security. India, as a major oil importer, may be indirectly affected by such geopolitical developments.
articles.raw_summary: The vessel was disabled by firing from a US warplane which was launched by American aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln.

articles.title: Oil prices slide as US, Iran close in on deal after 2+ months of flare-up
articles.summary: Iran is reviewing a new proposal from the US regarding a potential nuclear deal after over two months of tensions. The response will be conveyed via Pakistan. Oil prices have fallen as these negotiations progress, which could influence global energy markets and India's fuel import costs.
articles.raw_summary: Iran on Wednesday said it was reviewing a new US proposal and would convey its response soon via Pakistan.

articles.title: Indian-origin techie-doctor couple planning to move to Dubai amid Green Card struggles, 'We're just exhausted'
articles.summary: A tech professional and his cardiologist wife are contemplating a move to Dubai due to prolonged H-1B visa delays preventing family visits.

articles.title: NRI techie considers moving to India after 8 years amid stress, toxic work culture in US: ‘Completely lost and helpless’
articles.summary: An NRI technology professional in the US is considering resigning and moving to India due to stress and depression from a toxic work environment.
articles.raw_summary: An NRI technology professional in the US is considering resigning and moving to India due to stress and depression from a toxic work environment.

articles.title: Man makes fake Tinder profile of girlfriend after breakup, urges men to 'rape her'
articles.summary: A man created a fake Tinder profile impersonating his ex-girlfriend after their breakup. He falsely claimed she had 'rape fantasies' and that her refusal to consent was part of the fantasy. The incident raises concerns about digital harassment, impersonation, and gender-based violence in India.
articles.raw_summary: The accused, pretending to be his ex-girlfriend, told men about her 'rape fantasies' and that if she said 'no' to them, then it meant that she 'wanted more'.

articles.title: Genetic testing: Making advanced screening accessible to all
articles.summary: The article, authored by Chandra Ganjoo, Group CEO of Trivitron Healthcare, explores how genetic testing can be made more accessible to the public. It highlights the role of advanced screening in healthcare and suggests ways to improve availability. The piece emphasizes the need for broader adoption of genetic testing in India's healthcare system.

articles.title: From medical expertise to digital health fluency
articles.summary: Dr. Rajendra Pratap Gupta, Chairman of the Academy of Digital Health Sciences, writes about the need for medical professionals to develop digital health fluency. The article highlights the growing importance of integrating digital tools in healthcare delivery and policy. It emphasizes the shift from traditional medical expertise to a more technology-driven approach in India's healthcare system.
